articles.title: Positive conversations leave a temporary neural echo in mother-child brain networks
articles.summary: A brief, positive conversation between a mother and her child can align their brainwaves, and this neural synchronization lingers even after the interaction ends, suggesting that everyday chats may help shape the development of the social brain.
articles.aiSummary: A brief, positive conversation between a mother and her child can align their brainwaves, and this neural synchronization lingers even after the interaction ends, suggesting that everyday chats may help shape the development of the social brain.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A brief, positive conversation between a mother and her child can align their brainwaves, and this neural synchronization lingers even after the interaction ends, suggesting that everyday chats may help shape the development of the social brain.

articles.title: Harsh childhood environments shape future reproduction, but not always as evolutionary theory predicts
articles.summary: A new study using Canadian census data reveals that a neighborhood's economic and demographic conditions can predict the reproductive patterns of its residents 15 years later, offering insights into how childhood environments shape long-term family planning.
articles.aiSummary: A new study using Canadian census data reveals that a neighborhood's economic and demographic conditions can predict the reproductive patterns of its residents 15 years later, offering insights into how childhood environments shape long-term family planning.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A new study using Canadian census data reveals that a neighborhood's economic and demographic conditions can predict the reproductive patterns of its residents 15 years later, offering insights into how childhood environments shape long-term family planning.

articles.title: How your personal values change as you age, according to a large new study
articles.summary: Are you curious, or do you crave stability? A new psychological study of over 80,000 people reveals that looking at highly specific personal values can accurately predict a person's age.
articles.aiSummary: Are you curious, or do you crave stability? A new psychological study of over 80,000 people reveals that looking at highly specific personal values can accurately predict a person's age.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Are you curious, or do you crave stability? A new psychological study of over 80,000 people reveals that looking at highly specific personal values can accurately predict a person's age.

articles.title: Announcing Our Summer Book Club and Podcast
articles.summary: Each summer, we read a novel and explore its themes through conversations with leading thinkers at the intersection of science and culture. This summer, we’ll read Flights by Polish author Olga Tokarczuk, the recipient of the 2018 Nobel Prize in literature. Flights is an enigma of a novel—part memoir, part short story collection, part ethnography of travel—that explores the human impulse toward movement; the perpetual tension between exploring somewhere new or staying put. “Clearly I did not inherit whatever gene it is that makes it so when you linger in a place you start to put down roots,” T

articles.title: A common vitamin could help fight one of the deadliest brain cancers
articles.summary: A clinical trial is exploring whether high doses of vitamin B3 could give patients with glioblastoma a better chance against the aggressive brain cancer. Scientists found that niacin may help revive immune cells that tumors shut down, allowing them to attack cancer more effectively. Early results have been promising, with patients showing significantly better progression-free survival than expected.

articles.title: New psychology research finds a subtle link between speaking speed and politeness
articles.summary: How fast you speak affects how polite you sound. According to new research in Social Psychological and Personality Science, people spontaneously slow down to express courtesy, and listeners instinctively associate a slower voice with good manners.
articles.aiSummary: How fast you speak affects how polite you sound. According to new research in Social Psychological and Personality Science, people spontaneously slow down to express courtesy, and listeners instinctively associate a slower voice with good manners.

articles.title: Study finds biological age is the strongest predictor of how people view their dating prospects
articles.summary: Your age, income, and local economy heavily color how you view dating and job opportunities. A new study on online daters reveals how men and women perceive gender equity, career access, and romance in completely different ways.
articles.aiSummary: Your age, income, and local economy heavily color how you view dating and job opportunities. A new study on online daters reveals how men and women perceive gender equity, career access, and romance in completely different ways.

articles.title: Neuroscientists uncover how serotonin alters “belief stickiness”
articles.summary: A new study suggests serotonin helps the brain update its assumptions by reducing "belief stickiness." The findings explain how common antidepressants might relieve obsessive-compulsive traits by helping people let go of rigid, outdated thoughts.
articles.aiSummary: A new study suggests serotonin helps the brain update its assumptions by reducing "belief stickiness." The findings explain how common antidepressants might relieve obsessive-compulsive traits by helping people let go of rigid, outdated thoughts.

articles.title: Shockwaves from routine military duties associated with long-term anger and violence
articles.summary: Veterans who worked in high-blast occupations, such as artillery or weapons training, show higher rates of anger and aggression. Using artificial intelligence to scan medical records, researchers found this occupational exposure carries a distinct behavioral risk.
articles.aiSummary: Veterans who worked in high-blast occupations, such as artillery or weapons training, show higher rates of anger and aggression. Using artificial intelligence to scan medical records, researchers found this occupational exposure carries a distinct behavioral risk.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Veterans who worked in high-blast occupations, such as artillery or weapons training, show higher rates of anger and aggression. Using artificial intelligence to scan medical records, researchers found this occupational exposure carries a distinct behavioral risk.

articles.title: Voters rewrite past election predictions to protect their political identities
articles.summary: Voters often warp their memories of an election to align with the actual results. A new psychological study reveals how partisan biases shape our memories, leading people to subconsciously rewrite their past predictions to protect their political identities.
articles.aiSummary: Voters often warp their memories of an election to align with the actual results. A new psychological study reveals how partisan biases shape our memories, leading people to subconsciously rewrite their past predictions to protect their political identities.
articles.aiSummaryEn: Voters often warp their memories of an election to align with the actual results. A new psychological study reveals how partisan biases shape our memories, leading people to subconsciously rewrite their past predictions to protect their political identities.

articles.title: Study explores how attachment style, dark triad traits, and sexual coercion impact romantic couples
articles.summary: A new study reveals how attachment styles, personality traits, and aggressive behaviors influence romantic satisfaction. The findings show that women prioritize emotional bonds and partner traits, while men's relationship happiness is closely tied to experiences of sexual coercion.
articles.aiSummary: A new study reveals how attachment styles, personality traits, and aggressive behaviors influence romantic satisfaction. The findings show that women prioritize emotional bonds and partner traits, while men's relationship happiness is closely tied to experiences of sexual coercion.

articles.title: Scientists reprogram brain immune cells to fight Alzheimer’s
articles.summary: A newly identified molecule called OLE helped restore the brain’s immune cells to a more protective state in Alzheimer’s models. The treatment reduced toxic plaque buildup and improved memory, raising hopes for a new therapeutic approach.
articles.aiSummary: A newly identified molecule called OLE helped restore the brain’s immune cells to a more protective state in Alzheimer’s models. The treatment reduced toxic plaque buildup and improved memory, raising hopes for a new therapeutic approach.

articles.title: Lingering grief physically limits cardiovascular recovery after stress
articles.summary: A new study reveals that recalling a lost loved one physically spikes blood pressure. For those struggling with prolonged grief, the cardiovascular system fails to recover normally, suggesting an enduring link between emotional trauma and physical heart health.
articles.aiSummary: A new study reveals that recalling a lost loved one physically spikes blood pressure. For those struggling with prolonged grief, the cardiovascular system fails to recover normally, suggesting an enduring link between emotional trauma and physical heart health.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A new study reveals that recalling a lost loved one physically spikes blood pressure. For those struggling with prolonged grief, the cardiovascular system fails to recover normally, suggesting an enduring link between emotional trauma and physical heart health.

articles.title: The daily patterns of depression: How symptoms tend to co-occur over time
articles.summary: Researchers tracked over 300 young adults for a month to see how depression symptoms interact. They discovered that specific feelings, like anhedonia and sadness, act as central hubs that are strongly linked to a wider array of mental and physical symptoms.
articles.aiSummary: Researchers tracked over 300 young adults for a month to see how depression symptoms interact. They discovered that specific feelings, like anhedonia and sadness, act as central hubs that are strongly linked to a wider array of mental and physical symptoms.

articles.title: How Can We Feel Loved If We Don’t Feel Known?
articles.summary: For Sonja, researching happiness means being asked over and over again, “What is the secret to happiness?” Sonja has always dreaded that question. It’s ridiculous, it’s reductive, and it’s restrictive. But mostly she has dreaded it because she has never had a solid, science-based answer that she could offer with any confidence. There is no secret to happiness. But there are huge contributors to happiness, and some of them are more powerful than others. Sonja resists being pinned down to just one. But if she were pressed to give an answer, she now has one. She found it when she met Harry, who s

articles.title: The human brain nonconsciously filters out negative spoken words when distracted
articles.summary: A new study suggests the brain nonconsciously filters out emotionally negative spoken words when people are distracted. The findings indicate that the mind protects conscious awareness by ignoring disruptive emotional sounds.
articles.aiSummary: A new study suggests the brain nonconsciously filters out emotionally negative spoken words when people are distracted. The findings indicate that the mind protects conscious awareness by ignoring disruptive emotional sounds.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A new study suggests the brain nonconsciously filters out emotionally negative spoken words when people are distracted. The findings indicate that the mind protects conscious awareness by ignoring disruptive emotional sounds.

articles.title: Humans actively undermine AI lie detectors because they don’t want to accuse people of lying
articles.summary: A new psychology study reveals that when a highly accurate AI system confidently accuses someone of lying, human supervisors tend to override the decision—a "truth bias" that actively destroys the AI's detection accuracy.
articles.aiSummary: A new psychology study reveals that when a highly accurate AI system confidently accuses someone of lying, human supervisors tend to override the decision—a "truth bias" that actively destroys the AI's detection accuracy.
articles.aiSummaryEn: A new psychology study reveals that when a highly accurate AI system confidently accuses someone of lying, human supervisors tend to override the decision—a "truth bias" that actively destroys the AI's detection accuracy.

articles.title: Tubulin prevents toxic brain protein clumps linked to Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s
articles.summary: Scientists at Baylor College of Medicine may have uncovered a promising new way to combat Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s disease. Instead of trying to stop Tau and alpha-synuclein proteins from gathering into tiny droplets inside brain cells, the researchers found that tubulin—the protein that builds the cell’s internal transport network—can redirect these proteins away from forming toxic clumps and toward healthy, productive work.

articles.title: New study reveals how infants’ brains and bodies respond to music in the first year of life
articles.summary: New research reveals that while infants' brains process musical structure as early as three months old, their bodies take much longer to catch up. Structured physical movement to music doesn't emerge until closer to their first birthday.
articles.aiSummary: New research reveals that while infants' brains process musical structure as early as three months old, their bodies take much longer to catch up. Structured physical movement to music doesn't emerge until closer to their first birthday.

articles.title: Why American dads now spend more time on childcare than hunter-gatherer fathers
articles.summary: Fathers today spend four times as much time with their kids as they did 50 years ago. However, a parenting researcher notes this is largely because extended community and family support networks have deteriorated.
articles.aiSummary: Fathers today spend four times as much time with their kids as they did 50 years ago. However, a parenting researcher notes this is largely because extended community and family support networks have deteriorated.
